A1.label: 100000th prime via Sieve of Eratosthenes
A1.method: Sieve of Eratosthenes up to upper bound, extract 100000th prime

A3.label: 1299709 is prime (trial division)
A3.method: Trial division testing all factors up to √1299709

claim_formal.subject: the 100000th prime number
claim_formal.property: value of the 100000th prime in the sequence of primes (2, 3, 5, 7, 11, ...)
claim_formal.operator_note: 'exactly' means strict equality. The 100000th prime must be precisely 1299709. Primes are indexed starting at p(1)=2, p(2)=3, etc. — the standard convention.

adversarial_checks.question: Could the indexing convention differ (0-based vs 1-based)?
adversarial_checks.verification_performed: Checked standard mathematical convention: p(1)=2, p(2)=3, p(3)=5, ... The claim uses 'the 100000th prime' which in standard notation is p(100000). Verified sieve starts counting at p(1)=2.
adversarial_checks.finding: Sieve uses 1-based indexing (first prime counted is 2). Matches claim convention.

adversarial_checks.question: Is 1 sometimes counted as a prime, shifting the index?
adversarial_checks.verification_performed: Historically, 1 was sometimes considered prime, but modern convention (post-1800s) excludes 1. The sieve starts from 2. If 1 were included, p(100000) would be p(99999) in modern convention. Verified that the claim uses modern convention.
adversarial_checks.finding: Modern convention excludes 1 as prime. Both sieve and claim use this convention.

adversarial_checks.question: Could there be an off-by-one error in the sieve or counting?
adversarial_checks.verification_performed: Verified sieve against known small primes: p(1)=2, p(10)=29, p(100)=541, p(1000)=7919. Additionally, the independent π(x) counting function provides a structural cross-check: π(1299709)=100000 and π(1299708)=99999 together confirm 1299709 is the exact 100000th prime.
adversarial_checks.finding: Small-case verification and π(x) boundary check rule out off-by-one errors.
